locked_facts: Constructive interference: path difference Δ = nλ (phase difference = 2nπ), where n = 0, 1, 2, …
Destructive interference: path difference Δ = (n + ½)λ (phase difference = (2n+1)π)
For equal intensities I₁ = I₂ = I₀: I_max = 4I₀, I_min = 0 (complete cancellation)
Coherent sources: same frequency AND constant phase difference over time
Two independent light sources are NEVER coherent — they emit photons in random bursts
truth_statements: Interference is the superposition of two waves from coherent sources, producing a stable pattern of maxima and minima
Coherence requires both temporal coherence (monochromaticity) and spatial coherence (wavefronts in phase across the source)
Two independent light bulbs cannot produce a visible interference pattern because their relative phase changes randomly at ~10⁸ Hz
The resultant intensity at any point depends on the phase difference: I = I₁ + I₂ + 2√(I₁I₂)cosφ
Energy is not created or destroyed in interference — it is redistributed from dark fringes to bright fringes

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two coherent waves arrive at a point. When they are in phase (crest meets crest), the combined amplitude doubles and intensity quadruples to 4I₀. When they are out of phase by π (crest meets trough), they cancel completely to give zero intensity. Show the total energy across the pattern is conserved — bright fringes gain what dark fringes lose.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Animated wave superposition: two sinusoidal waves sliding into alignment (constructive) and then shifting by half wavelength (destructive), with intensity meter going from 4I₀ to 0
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that two light beams CAN produce darkness — this violates the intuition that 'more light = always brighter'

simulation_states.label: Two separate wave sources
simulation_states.description: Two point sources emitting circular wavefronts of the same wavelength. The sources are incoherent — their phases jump randomly. The combined pattern on a detection screen fluctuates randomly with no stable fringes.
simulation_states.emphasis: Without coherence, no stable interference pattern forms — just uniform illumination on average

simulation_states.label: Coherent sources — fixed phase relationship
simulation_states.description: The two sources now have a fixed phase relationship (derived from the same parent source). Their wavefronts overlap predictably. Some regions show reinforcement, others show cancellation.
simulation_states.emphasis: Coherence means constant phase difference — this is what makes a stable pattern possible

simulation_states.label: Constructive and destructive interference
simulation_states.description: At points where path difference = nλ, crests meet crests: intensity = I_max = (√I₁+√I₂)². At points where path difference = (n+½)λ, crests meet troughs: intensity = I_min = (√I₁−√I₂)². For equal intensities: I_max = 4I₀, I_min = 0.
simulation_states.emphasis: Constructive: Δ = nλ → bright. Destructive: Δ = (n+½)λ → dark. Total energy is conserved across the pattern.

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts the intensity ratio I₁/I₂ and the phase difference φ. When I₁ = I₂, I_min = 0 (perfect dark fringes). When I₁ ≠ I₂, I_min > 0 (no complete darkness). Phase slider moves between constructive and destructive interference in real time.
simulation_states.emphasis: See how unequal intensities prevent complete cancellation — and why equal-intensity sources give the clearest fringes

common_misconceptions.belief: Two light bulbs can produce an interference pattern if placed close together
common_misconceptions.correction: Two independent light sources are never coherent — each atom emits light in random bursts lasting ~10⁻⁸ s, so the phase difference changes randomly ~10⁸ times per second. No detector can track this, so the pattern averages out to uniform illumination. Coherent sources must be derived from the SAME parent source (e.g., via a double slit).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate rapidly flickering random-phase sources with the detection screen showing no stable fringes, then switch to coherent sources showing a clear stable pattern

common_misconceptions.belief: In destructive interference, energy is destroyed — light + light = darkness means energy is lost
common_misconceptions.correction: Energy is NEVER destroyed in interference. It is redistributed — the energy missing from dark fringes reappears in bright fringes. The average intensity across the entire pattern equals I₁ + I₂, exactly as if there were no interference.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy bar graph: total energy across all fringes = constant. Bright fringes have extra energy; dark fringes have none. Sum is always conserved.

legend.meaning: Intensities of the two individual waves (W/m²)
Path difference between the two waves (m)
